query: Perry separator vs other separators in OB/GYN procedures

retrieved_docs.title: Vacuum Extraction -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: Vacuum extractors often have replaced forceps when assistance is required for vaginal delivery. Compared with metal-cup vacuum extractors, soft-cup devices cause fewer neonatal scalp injuries and are easier to use; however, they detach more often.

retrieved_docs.title: Vacuum Extraction -- Indications -- Classification of Operative Vaginal Deliveries
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction is classified by the status of the fetal head at the time of vacuum application and the degree of rotation necessary. Outlet delivery The scalp is visible at the introitus without separating the labia. The fetal skull has reached the pelvic floor. The fetal head is at or on the perineum. The sagittal suture is in AP or ROA/LOA or OP position. Rotation does not exceed 45 degrees. Low delivery The leading point of the fetal skull is at station +2 or more and not on the pelvic floor. Without rotation: Rotation is 45 degrees or less With rotation: Rotation is greater than 45 degrees Mid delivery The station is above +2, but the head is engaged.

retrieved_docs.title: Vacuum Extraction -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Should the device disengage during delivery attempts, additional attempts may be initiated. Manufacturers discourage more than two to three attempts (or pop-offs), and clinicians should proceed with cesarean section. [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Vacuum Extraction -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The maximum time to safely complete a vacuum extraction and the acceptable number of detachments are unknown. It is recommended that vacuum-assisted deliveries be achieved with no more than three sets of pulls and a maximum of two to three cup detachments or pop-offs. The total vacuum application time should be limited to 20 to 30 minutes. Sequential application of the vacuum and the forceps or vice versa is discouraged due to a higher risk of fetal and maternal injury.

retrieved_docs.title: Hysteroscopy -- Equipment -- Tissue Retrieval Systems
retrieved_docs.content: Several types of tissue retrieval systems on the market easily fragment and extract intrauterine pathology. These devices may be referred to as mechanical hysteroscopic tissue removal systems or mHTR systems. These systems overcome the limitations of traditional resectoscopic systems and methods because they do not use electrocautery to remove the pathology, thereby eliminating the risk of electrosurgical damage to surrounding structures and energetic reactions, including:

retrieved_docs.title: Ex utero Intrapartum Treatment (EXIT) Procedure -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: EXIT-to-resection and EXIT-to-separation of congenital twins may require additional surgical equipment specific to the procedure being performed. EXIT-to-ECMO procedures require an ECMO unit and trained support staff. EXIT-to-airway procedures may also require the following supplies: Pediatric airway cart Pediatric laryngoscopes Endotracheal tubes: 3.0 mm cuffed, 3.0 mm uncuffed, 2.5 mm uncuffed Pediatric airway cart with supplies for direct laryngoscopy and removal of tracheal clip or balloon if indicated Pediatric tracheostomy tray

retrieved_docs.title: Oophorectomy -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: The following equipment is typically required for performing a laparoscopic oophorectomy: Laparoscopic monitor Laparoscope (5/10 mm, 0/30 degrees) Carbon dioxide source and tubing for insufflation Two 5 mm trocars One 5 mm to 12 mm trocar (umbilical, larger if removal of the adnexal mass is required) Atraumatic graspers Electrocautery or ligation device Endoscopic retrieval bag Scalpel (#11 or #15 blade) Forceps Needle driver Absorbable sutures Incision dressing of choice

retrieved_docs.title: Vacuum Extraction -- Clinical Significance -- Vacuum Extractors
retrieved_docs.content: Easy to learn Quick delivery Less neonatal craniofacial injury Less maternal discomfort Less maternal genital trauma Less anesthesia required

retrieved_docs.title: Ex utero Intrapartum Treatment (EXIT) Procedure -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: Preparing the operating room and the involved medical and surgical teams is paramount on the day of the procedure. The operating room should be prepared with the required equipment as indicated by the specific EXIT type. Ideally, the teams are composed of experienced clinicians who work together often and have some continuity. Rehearsal of the procedure with all team members involved is usually performed before the actual procedure. Preparation for massive bleeding and transfusion is critical as the average maternal blood loss during EXIT has been estimated at 1 L. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Oophorectomy -- Personnel
retrieved_docs.content: The following personnel are typically involved in a laparoscopic oophorectomy procedure: Operating surgeon Surgical assistant Scrub technician or scrub nurse

retrieved_docs.title: Hysteroscopy -- Equipment -- Tissue Retrieval Systems
retrieved_docs.content: Truclear 8.0 system : FDA approved in 2005, this mHTR is manufactured by Medtronic, is 9.0 mm in diameter, has a 0-degree optic device, a 4.0 mm disposable cutting device, and an operator to set window closure.

retrieved_docs.title: Forceps Delivery -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Forceps allow for an alternative to vacuum-assisted delivery or cesarean section particularly with a non-reassuring tracing when delivery is imminent, maternal exhaustion from pushing, or medical comorbidities that preclude pushing or operative delivery. Advantages of forceps as compared to vacuum include: Unlikely to detach from head Can be used in premature fetuses Allows rotation Less bleeding from the scalp Less encephalopathy In general, vacuum extraction is safer than forceps for the mother while forceps is safer than vacuum extraction for the fetus.

retrieved_docs.title: Vacuum Extraction -- Personnel
retrieved_docs.content: Operator and at least one assistant should be present at the time of vacuum-assisted vaginal delivery. In addition, personnel should be available to assist with infant resuscitation if needed.

retrieved_docs.title: Tubal Ligation (Archived) -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: For a postpartum or postabortion procedure, access to the Fallopian tube is achieved using a mini-laparotomy incision. A scalpel, several small retractors (such as a Senn or Army-Navy retractor), and surgical scissors (such as Metzenbaum or Mayo scissors) will suffice. A suction tip, such as a Yankauer, may also be utilized. A clamp such as a Babcock is again used to grasp the tube once it is in the surgical incision, and the chosen technique for tubal ligation can take place.

retrieved_docs.title: Hysteroscopy -- Equipment -- Tissue Retrieval Systems
retrieved_docs.content: Synphioin system : FDA approved in 2014, this mHTR is manufactured by Boston Scientific and Minerva Surgical, is 6.3 mm in diameter, has a 0-degree optic device, a 3.6 mm disposable cutting device, and automatic window closure. Unique to this device is the capability for radiofrequency bipolar plasma tissue resection.

retrieved_docs.title: Abdominal Hysterectomy -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: The following equipment is required: Long angled #3 knife handle and blade curved and straight Zepplin forceps Kelly forceps Tonsil forceps long Allis clamps curved and straight Mayo uterine scissors Jorgenson scissors sponge forceps self-retaining retractor Deaver retractors Richardson retractors malleable retractors short and long needle holders sutures electrosurgical device suction apparatus

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 1021
retrieved_docs.content: organizes general obstetric procedure categories (إجراء توليدي, Antepartum Procedures, Obstetrics incision) and specific clinical actions—ranging from diagnostic sampling and membrane management to fetal stimulation, reduction of multiple gestations, and surgical treatment of ectopic pregnancies—providing a comprehensive framework for coordinating obstetric care across the ante‑partum, intrapartum, and postpartum periods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 1102
retrieved_docs.content: The collection of gynecologic interventions is organized under the overarching category “عملية للأعضاء التناسلية الأنثوية” (procedures for female genital organs). Within this category, major surgical procedures include “إزالة الرحم” (hysterectomy) and “استئصال المبيض” (ovarian removal), as well as “توسيع الرحم وكشطه” (uterine dilation and curettage). “توسيع الرحم وكشطه” further comprises specific techniques such as “Dilatation and curettage (D&C); aspiration after delivery or abortion” and its child procedure “شفط بالتخلية” (suction curettage). The procedure “Aspiration curettage following delivery or abortion” is classified as “Dilatation and curettage (D&C); aspiration after delivery or abortion,” indicating that it is a particular form of D&C performed after delivery or abortion. This hierarchical organization clarifies how various gynecologic surgeries are related, supporting clinicians in selecting appropriate interventions and understanding their place within comprehensive female reproductive care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 151
retrieved_docs.content: are also categorized under “other non‑therapeutic procedures; female organs,” emphasizing their procedural rather than therapeutic nature. Vacuum dilatation of the cervix is classified both by the device involved and as a member of the “other non‑therapeutic procedures; female organs” class, highlighting its dual classification based on technique and anatomical focus. Vacuum aspiration (شفط بالتخلية) belongs to the therapeutic obstetric procedures category, underscoring its use as an active treatment method in obstetrics. Collectively, these entities demonstrate a structured hierarchy in which specific obstetric and cervical interventions are organized by therapeutic intent, procedural type, anatomical site, and the devices employed, facilitating clearer clinical understanding and more coordinated patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2069
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction is a broad medical concept that refers to the removal of a tissue, organ, or fetus, and the specific obstetric technique of الولادة عن طريق شفط الجنين (vacuum‑assisted delivery) represents a narrower, specialized form of this procedure. In this context, الولادة عن طريق شفط الجنين is understood as a particular type of extraction employed to assist fetal delivery by applying suction, illustrating how a general extraction principle can be applied in a specific clinical scenario.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1790
retrieved_docs.content: Routine obstetric care, which includes comprehensive antepartum care, vaginal delivery (with or without episiotomy and/or forceps), and postpartum care, is broadly related to assisted delivery techniques such as forceps, vacuum extraction, and breech delivery, highlighting the integration of standard obstetric management with these specialized delivery methods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2294
retrieved_docs.content: Combined obstetrical version is a broad category of obstetric procedures that encompasses the specific technique known as internal and combined version without extraction. The internal and combined version without extraction is identified as a subtype of combined obstetrical version and is classified under the broader group of other delivery procedures, indicating its role as an alternative method used in the management of childbirth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 894
retrieved_docs.content: the community’s medical knowledge connects vacuum extraction, forceps delivery, other partial breech extraction, obstetric manipulations, routine obstetric care, and related newborn screening classifications, providing a comprehensive framework for clinical documentation and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 959
retrieved_docs.content: Dilation of cervix uteri and vacuum aspiration of products of conception from uterus is classified as a عملية في عنق الرحم (procedure in the cervix) and serves as a method for تفريغ منتجات الحمل المحتجزة (evacuation of retained pregnancy products). This specific cervical intervention therefore belongs to the broader category of cervical medical procedures and is used to remove retained products of conception from the uterus. In the same classification hierarchy, Medical procedure on cervix is also linked to عملية في عنق الرحم, underscoring that both the English‑language description and the Arabic term refer to the same group of cervical procedures employed in obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 1072
retrieved_docs.content: delivery and include the narrower method of delivery by fetal suction. Operations to facilitate delivery are defined as types of cesarean delivery, reinforcing the central role of surgical birth techniques. Together, these interconnected entities and relationships provide a comprehensive framework for understanding the clinical, procedural, and data‑driven aspects of labor, delivery, and related obstetric conditions, supporting informed decision‑making and optimized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1931
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction is a procedure that can be performed using the method الولادة القيصرية (cesarean delivery). Within the spectrum of extraction techniques, الولادة عن طريق شفط الجنين (vacuum‑assisted delivery) represents a narrower, more specific form of extraction. Routine obstetric care—including antepartum care, vaginal delivery (with or without episiotomy and/or forceps), and postpartum care—has a direct relationship with الولادة القيصرية, indicating that cesarean delivery is one of the delivery options considered in standard obstetric management. The same routine obstetric care also has a broader relationship with الولادة عن طريق شفط الجنين, reflecting that vacuum‑assisted delivery is encompassed within the comprehensive range of services provided in routine obstetric care. Together, these connections illustrate how different delivery methods, from cesarean sections to vacuum extraction, are integrated into both specific extraction procedures and the overall framework of routine obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1014
retrieved_docs.content: Aspiration curettage following delivery or abortion is classified as dilatation and curettage (D&C) aspiration after delivery or abortion. The procedural category dilatation and curettage (D&C) aspiration after delivery or abortion likewise defines itself as dilatation and curettage (d&c) aspiration after delivery or abortion, indicating that both terms refer to the same obstetric surgical technique used to remove retained products of conception after childbirth or induced abortion. This unified classification supports consistent clinical documentation, coding, and selection of appropriate management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 1 -- Community 83
retrieved_docs.content: The community organizes obstetric and perinatal care within a hierarchical procedural taxonomy called “إجراء توليدي” (obstetric procedural category), which is a child of the broader “Female genital and obstetric procedures” category that encompasses all gynecologic and obstetric interventions. Within إجراء توليدي, Antepartum Procedures form a major branch; the sub‑branch “Antepartum application, insertion or removal procedures” groups invasive actions such as amniocentesis (بزل السائل الأمنيوسي), chorionic villus sampling (خزعة الزغابات المشيمية), fetal reduction, and device insertion or removal. Intrapartum and post‑partum interventions include amniotomy at delivery, manual fetal rotation, intrapartum fetal stimulation, amnioinfusion, cesarean hysterectomy, cardiac puncture (البزل القلبي), uterine curettage, evacuation of retained products of conception, vaginal delivery (بضع الفرج), vaginal birth after cesarean, and vaginal assisted delivery, all linked to obstetrics incision

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1638
retrieved_docs.content: Cesarean hysterectomy and Intrapartum Fetal Stimulation are both classified as إجراء توليدي (obstetric procedures). Within the broader category of إجراء توليدي, there is a child concept termed تفريغ منتجات الحمل المحتجزة (removal of retained products of conception). The specific procedure Dilation of cervix uteri and vacuum aspiration of products of conception from uterus is identified as a type of تفريغ منتجات الحمل المحتجزة, illustrating that it is a particular method used to accomplish the removal of retained products of conception. This hierarchy shows how Cesarean hysterectomy and Intrapartum Fetal Stimulation belong to the general group of obstetric interventions, while dilation of the cervix with vacuum aspiration represents a specialized technique under the subcategory of retained‑product removal, highlighting the organization of related obstetric practices for clinical understanding and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 752
retrieved_docs.content: essential for clinicians to anticipate potential delivery complications, decide when normal delivery procedures are feasible, and implement appropriate interventions to optimize maternal and neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2909
retrieved_docs.content: Delivery by double application of forceps is a obstetric procedure in which the fetal head serves as the direct site of the intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2791
retrieved_docs.content: Episiotomy and تمزق الأغشية الاصطناعي (artificial membrane rupture) are both classified under الإجراءات العلاجية الخاصة بالتوليد (obstetric therapeutic procedures). By recognizing these interventions as subcomponents of the broader category of obstetric therapeutic care, clinicians can better organize treatment options, understand their related indications, and apply appropriate management strategies within the field of obstetrics.
